skill.name: gpt-image-edit
skill.description: Edit images with OpenAI GPT Image 2 (the `/edit` endpoint of ChatGPT Images 2.0) on RunComfy — bundled with the model's documented prompting patterns so the skill gets sharper output than naive prompting against the same model. Documents GPT Image Edit's strengths (preservation language, multilingual in-image text editing, multi-reference up to 10 images, layout / typography precision), the schema, and when to route to Nano Banana Edit / Flux Kontext / GPT Image 2 t2i instead. Calls `runcomfy run openai/gpt-image-2/edit` through the local RunComfy CLI. Triggers on "gpt image edit", "gpt-image-edit", "chatgpt image edit", "edit with gpt image 2", or any explicit ask to edit with this model.
skill.summary: Documentation-only skill that guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code on how to invoke the GPT Image 2 `/edit` endpoint via the RunComfy CLI with optimized preservation, multilingual, and multi-reference prompts.
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: The file is pure markdown documentation with no executable code. All 59 'external_commands' findings are markdown backtick formatting for inline code spans (e.g., `runcomfy run openai/gpt-image-2/edit`), not actual shell backtick execution. The 11 'network' findings are documentation URLs (runcomfy.com, docs.runcomfy.com, model-api.runcomfy.net) that are explicitly disclosed in the Security & Privacy section as the documented outbound endpoints. The 5 'path traversal' findings are literal `<absolute/path>` placeholder strings in user-facing CLI examples, not real traversal sequences. The 'hidden file' finding refers to `~/.config/runcomfy/token.json`, which the skill explicitly documents as mode-0600 token storage. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings are misidentifications of the documented `size` parameter enum (3 fixed values + auto). No prompt injection attempts, no data exfiltration, no obfuscation detected. Risk remains low because the skill instructs users to invoke a third-party CLI (`runcomfy`) that performs outbound HTTPS calls and writes files to disk, but these behaviors are explicitly documented.

low_findings.title: Outbound network calls to third-party CLI (RunComfy)
low_findings.description: The skill instructs Claude/Codex/Claude Code to invoke `runcomfy run openai/gpt-image-2/edit`, which POSTs to `https://model-api.runcomfy.net` and downloads outputs to disk. User prompts and image URLs are transmitted to RunComfy's hosted model API. This is the documented, intended behavior of the skill and is fully disclosed in the Security & Privacy section. No hidden network endpoints detected. Risk is low because the user has explicitly opted in by installing the RunComfy CLI and authenticating with their own token.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The outbound endpoint is explicitly documented in the Security & Privacy section. No obfuscation or hidden calls are present. Confidence is high that the disclosed behavior matches the actual behavior.

low_findings.title: File writes to user-controlled output directory
low_findings.description: Examples use `--output-dir <absolute/path>` to write generated images to a user-specified directory. The `~/.config/runcomfy/token.json` file stores the API token with mode 0600. Both are disclosed in the Security & Privacy section. No path traversal is constructed by the skill itself — the user supplies the path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Documented filesystem behavior with explicit user consent. The CLI's 2 GiB download cap is a reasonable safeguard against disk-fill attacks.

content.user_title: Edit Images with GPT Image 2 via RunComfy
content.value_statement: Naive prompts against GPT Image 2 drift on identity, brand, and multilingual text. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code the documented preservation language, multi-reference numbering, and script-quoting patterns that produce sharp, on-brief edits on the first try.
actual_capabilities: Instructs Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to invoke `runcomfy run openai/gpt-image-2/edit` with a valid JSON input body
Provides the documented input schema: prompt (string), images (up to 10 HTTPS URLs), size (auto, 1024_1024, 1024_1536, 1536_1024)
Teaches preservation-first prompt structure: lead with what to keep, end with the change
Provides multilingual in-image text rewriting patterns with script naming and character quoting for kana, CJK, Cyrillic, and Arabic
Documents multi-reference composition with numbered cues ('subject from image 1, lighting from image 2')
Routes the user to the right sibling skill (gpt-image-2 t2i, Nano Banana Edit, Flux Kontext) based on the actual task
limitations: Documentation only — does not execute the RunComfy CLI itself; the user must install and authenticate it
`size` accepts only 3 fixed values plus `auto`; any other value returns 422
`images` is capped at 10 URLs, with the first as primary and the rest as auxiliary cues
Long compound edit instructions drift; this skill advises splitting into multiple passes rather than baking in a workaround

use_cases.title: Multilingual ad localization
use_cases.description: Translate the in-image headline of a campaign asset into Japanese, Cyrillic, or Arabic while keeping the photograph, layout, and brand mark identical.

use_cases.title: Background swap with full identity preservation
use_cases.description: Replace the background of a product or portrait shot while keeping the face, pose, clothing, and brand mark exactly as in the source.

use_cases.title: Multi-reference composition
use_cases.description: Combine a subject from one image with a room, lighting, or palette from another image using numbered reference cues.

prompt_templates.title: Background swap with identity preservation
prompt_templates.prompt:
Use the gpt-image-edit skill to swap the background while preserving the subject.

Input image: <HTTPS URL of the source image>
Edit instruction: "Keep the person, pose, face, clothing, and brand mark unchanged. Replace the background with a soft warm-grey studio sweep and a gentle floor shadow."
Output directory: <absolute path>
prompt_templates.scenario: Single-reference preservation edit for product or portrait shots.

prompt_templates.title: Multilingual headline rewrite
prompt_templates.prompt:
Use the gpt-image-edit skill to rewrite only the in-image headline in a new language.

Input image: <HTTPS URL of the source poster>
Edit instruction: "Keep the photograph, layout, lighting, and brand mark exactly as in the input. Replace only the in-image headline. The new headline reads 'コーヒー' in bold Japanese kana, same position and font weight as before."
Output directory: <absolute path>
prompt_templates.scenario: Localize a campaign asset into Japanese, Cyrillic, or Arabic.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-reference composition
prompt_templates.prompt:
Use the gpt-image-edit skill to compose a subject from one image into a scene from another.

Images: ["<HTTPS URL of subject>", "<HTTPS URL of room/scene>"]
Edit instruction: "Compose subject from image 1 into the room from image 2. Match the lighting and color palette of image 2. Keep image 1 subject identity (face, pose, clothing) unchanged."
Output directory: <absolute path>
prompt_templates.scenario: Combine subject identity with a different environment.

prompt_templates.title: Layout-precise repositioning
prompt_templates.prompt:
Use the gpt-image-edit skill to move an in-image element with directional language.

Input image: <HTTPS URL of the source>
Edit instruction: "Keep everything else unchanged. Move the headline from top-right to bottom-center, preserving font weight and size."
Output directory: <absolute path>
prompt_templates.scenario: Adjust layout or CTA placement without redrawing the rest.

output_examples.input: Source: portrait photo on a cluttered outdoor background. Instruction: 'Keep the person, pose, face, clothing, and brand mark unchanged. Replace the background with a soft warm-grey studio sweep and a gentle floor shadow.'
output: Subject identity (face, pose, clothing, brand mark) preserved
Background replaced with soft warm-grey studio sweep
Gentle floor shadow added at the base
Output written to the specified --output-dir as a generated image

output_examples.input: Source: English poster with the headline 'GRAND OPENING'. Instruction: 'Keep the photograph, layout, lighting, and brand mark exactly as in the input. Replace only the in-image headline. The new headline reads "コーヒー" in bold Japanese kana, same position and font weight as before.'
output: Photograph, layout, lighting, and brand mark unchanged
Headline replaced with コーヒー in bold Japanese kana
Output written to the specified --output-dir as a generated image

output_examples.input: Two source images: image 1 is a person in casual clothing; image 2 is a sunlit kitchen. Instruction: 'Compose subject from image 1 into the room from image 2. Match the lighting and color palette of image 2. Keep image 1 subject identity unchanged.'
output: Subject from image 1 composed into the kitchen from image 2
Lighting and color palette matched to image 2
Subject face, pose, and clothing preserved
Output written to the specified --output-dir as a generated image

best_practices: Always lead the prompt with a preservation clause: 'Keep [face, pose, clothing, brand, framing] unchanged' before stating the change.
Quote the target text in its actual script and name the script explicitly (kana, CJK, Cyrillic, Arabic) rather than paraphrasing in Latin characters.
Use numbered references when passing multiple images: 'subject from image 1, lighting from image 2, palette from image 3'.
Prefer `size: 'auto'` to preserve the input aspect ratio; only override when the edit explicitly changes framing.
anti_patterns: Stacking many edits into one long compound instruction ('change A and B and C and D') — drift grows with each added scope, so split into multiple passes instead.
Omitting the preservation clause — without it, the model subtly rewrites the face, brand, or framing.
Paraphrasing the in-image text in Latin characters instead of quoting the actual glyphs in the target script — the rendered text will not match.
Passing more than 10 image URLs or requesting a `size` outside the three fixed values plus `auto` — both return 422.

faq.question: What is the difference between this skill and the gpt-image-2 t2i skill?
faq.answer: This skill wraps the `/edit` endpoint for image-to-image edits, including preservation, multilingual text rewriting, and multi-reference composition. The gpt-image-2 t2i sibling skill is for generating new images from a text prompt with no source image.

faq.question: How is the API token stored?
faq.answer: `runcomfy login` writes the token to `~/.config/runcomfy/token.json` with mode 0600 (owner-only). In CI or containers, set the `RUNCOMFY_TOKEN` environment variable instead of using the file.

faq.question: Is there a risk of shell injection from the prompt content?
faq.answer: No. The user prompt is passed as a JSON string via the `--input` flag, and the RunComfy CLI transmits the JSON body directly to the Model API over HTTPS without shell expansion.

faq.question: Which image URLs are fetched and by whom?
faq.answer: Image, mask, and video URLs are fetched by the RunComfy model server, not by the CLI on your machine. Treat external URLs as untrusted; image-based prompt injection is a known risk for any image-edit model.

faq.question: What outbound endpoints does the skill use?
faq.answer: Only `model-api.runcomfy.net` for request submission and the `*.runcomfy.net` / `*.runcomfy.com` download whitelist for generated outputs. There is no telemetry or third-party callback.

faq.question: Is there a cap on generated file size?
faq.answer: Yes. The CLI aborts any single download larger than 2 GiB to prevent disk-fill from a malicious or runaway model output.
